content.text: An index is the data structure that sits between raw documents and a query engine. When you call
chunks using a node parser (default: 1024-token chunks with 20-token overlap).
Each chunk is embedded into a vector using the configured embedding model.
Chunks and their vectors are persisted in the backing vector store.
The index is also the entry point for retrieval: calling
returns objects that search the stored nodes.
LlamaIndex ships four index types: VectorStoreIndex, SummaryIndex, KeywordTableIndex, and KnowledgeGraphIndex. VectorStoreIndex covers 90% of use cases.

The most widely used index. Each chunk becomes one embedding vector. Retrieval is approximate nearest-neighbour search (ANN) over those vectors. By default the vectors are stored in-memory using SimpleVectorStore; for persistence, plug in any vector store.

# pip install llama-index-core llama-index-llms-openai llama-index-embeddings-openai
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader, Settings
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

Settings.llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./data").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents, show_progress=True)

# Persist to disk so you don't re-embed on restart
index.storage_context.persist(persist_dir="./index_store")

from llama_index.core import StorageContext, load_index_from_storage, Settings
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI

Settings.llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")

storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(persist_dir="./index_store")
index = load_index_from_storage(storage_context)

response = index.as_query_engine().query("What is the main topic?")
print(response)
For production, replace the in-memory store with a persistent vector database. The index API stays the same — only the

# pip install llama-index-vector-stores-qdrant qdrant-client
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.vector_stores.qdrant import QdrantVectorStore
import qdrant_client

client = qdrant_client.QdrantClient(host="localhost", port=6333)
vector_store = QdrantVectorStore(client=client, collection_name="my_docs")
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    storage_context=storage_context,
    show_progress=True,
)

# Reload later: the collection is already in Qdrant
index2 = VectorStoreIndex.from_vector_store(vector_store)
query_engine = index2.as_query_engine()
The default node parser splits on token count. LlamaIndex ships several alternatives that produce better chunks for specific document types.
Small chunks with sentence context window for re-ranking
Long documents where topics shift gradually
Builds parent-child chunk tree for recursive retrieval

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceSplitter

# Use a sentence-aware splitter with 512-token chunks
splitter = SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64)

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    transformations=[splitter],   # replaces the default splitter
)
Sentence Window — Small Chunk, Wide Context
Retrieve small chunks for high relevance, but pass larger context to the LLM.
wraps each sentence in a window of N surrounding sentences, stored as metadata. At query time, a
swaps the small sentence back out for its wider window before the LLM sees it.

from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceWindowNodeParser
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import MetadataReplacementPostProcessor
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex

node_parser = SentenceWindowNodeParser.from_defaults(
    window_size=3,      # sentences before and after the anchor
    window_metadata_key="window",
    original_text_metadata_key="original_text",
)

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    transformations=[node_parser],
)

# At query time, replace sentence with window
query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    node_postprocessors=[
        MetadataReplacementPostProcessor(target_metadata_key="window")
    ]
)
response = query_engine.query("What is the capital?")

SummaryIndex stores all nodes in a flat list without embedding them. When queried, it iterates through nodes in order, asking the LLM to progressively summarise. Use it for questions that require synthesising the entire document rather than retrieving specific passages.
'Summarise this 50-page report', 'What are all the risks mentioned?'
'What revenue figure appears on page 12?' — linear scan is slow and expensive.

from llama_index.core import SummaryIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./report").load_data()
index = SummaryIndex.from_documents(documents)

# Tree summarise: LLM summarises batches of nodes, then summarises summaries
query_engine = index.as_query_engine(response_mode="tree_summarize")
response = query_engine.query("What are the main findings of this report?")
print(response)

KnowledgeGraphIndex extracts (subject, predicate, object) triples from documents using an LLM and stores them in a graph structure. Retrieval queries the graph to find related entities, then uses the surrounding triples as context.
This is LlamaIndex's built-in approach to graph RAG. For production, pair it with a Neo4j or NetworkX backend.

# pip install llama-index-graph-stores-neo4j
from llama_index.core import KnowledgeGraphIndex
from llama_index.graph_stores.neo4j import Neo4jGraphStore

graph_store = Neo4jGraphStore(
    username="neo4j",
    password="password",
    url="bolt://localhost:7687",
    database="neo4j",
)

from llama_index.core import StorageContext
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(graph_store=graph_store)

index = KnowledgeGraphIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    storage_context=storage_context,
    max_triplets_per_chunk=5,      # LLM extracts up to 5 triples per chunk
    include_embeddings=True,        # also store embeddings for hybrid search
)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    include_text=True,             # include surrounding text, not just triples
    response_mode="tree_summarize",
)
response = query_engine.query("Who is the CEO of Acme Corp?")
Indexes support incremental updates without full rebuilds. Inserting new documents or deleting stale ones is efficient for corpora that change frequently.

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, Document

# Insert a new document
new_doc = Document(
    text="Q4 2024 revenue reached $2.5B, up 19% year-over-year.",
    metadata={"source": "earnings-q4-2024.pdf"},
)
index.insert(new_doc)

# Delete by document ID
index.delete_ref_doc("earnings-q3-2024.pdf", delete_from_docstore=True)

# Refresh: re-index changed documents, skip unchanged ones
refreshed = index.refresh_ref_docs(
    [new_doc, existing_doc_that_may_have_changed],
)
print(f"Refreshed: {sum(refreshed)} of {len(refreshed)} documents updated")
Every node inherits its parent document's metadata. At retrieval time, pass a
object to restrict the ANN search to nodes matching specific metadata values. This is more efficient than post-filtering because the vector store applies the filter before the ANN search.

from llama_index.core.vector_stores import MetadataFilters, MetadataFilter, FilterOperator

filters = MetadataFilters(filters=[
    MetadataFilter(key="department", value="finance"),
    MetadataFilter(
        key="year",
        value=2024,
        operator=FilterOperator.GTE,   # year >= 2024
    ),
])

retriever = index.as_retriever(
    similarity_top_k=5,
    filters=filters,
)
nodes = retriever.retrieve("quarterly revenue")
for n in nodes:
    print(n.score, n.metadata["source"], n.text[:80])
Exact keyword search, no embedding budget
A common failure mode: small chunks retrieve precisely but lack context, while large chunks have context but are noisy retrieval targets.
solves this by creating two node sizes: small child nodes for retrieval and larger parent nodes for generation. After retrieving a small node, LlamaIndex swaps in the parent node's text.

from llama_index.core.node_parser import HierarchicalNodeParser, get_leaf_nodes
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.core.retrievers import AutoMergingRetriever
from llama_index.core.storage.docstore import SimpleDocumentStore

# Creates nodes at 3 sizes: 2048 -> 512 -> 128 tokens
node_parser = HierarchicalNodeParser.from_defaults(
    chunk_sizes=[2048, 512, 128]
)
all_nodes = node_parser.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)
leaf_nodes = get_leaf_nodes(all_nodes)   # the smallest (128-token) nodes

# Store ALL nodes (leaf + parent) in the docstore
docstore = SimpleDocumentStore()
docstore.add_documents(all_nodes)

storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(docstore=docstore)

# Index only the leaf nodes for retrieval
index = VectorStoreIndex(leaf_nodes, storage_context=storage_context)

# AutoMergingRetriever: if >50% of a parent's children are retrieved,
# replace them all with the parent node
base_retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=12)
retriever = AutoMergingRetriever(
    base_retriever,
    storage_context,
    verbose=True,
)
query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine.from_args(retriever)
Real-world pipelines often have multiple indexes — one per data source, one per department, or one per document type. LlamaIndex's
let you query across indexes with a single call.
RouterQueryEngine — LLM Routes to the Right Index

from llama_index.core.tools import QueryEngineTool
from llama_index.core.query_engine import RouterQueryEngine
from llama_index.core.selectors import LLMSingleSelector

# Two indexes with different content
finance_engine = finance_index.as_query_engine()
hr_engine = hr_index.as_query_engine()

tools = [
    QueryEngineTool.from_defaults(
        query_engine=finance_engine,
        description="Use this for questions about revenue, margins, earnings, or financial metrics.",
    ),
    QueryEngineTool.from_defaults(
        query_engine=hr_engine,
        description="Use this for questions about HR policies, benefits, leave, or org structure.",
    ),
]

router = RouterQueryEngine(
    selector=LLMSingleSelector.from_defaults(),
    query_engine_tools=tools,
    verbose=True,
)

# LLM reads descriptions and routes to the right engine
response = router.query("What is the parental leave policy?")
# -> routes to hr_engine
response2 = router.query("What was the gross margin in Q3?")
# -> routes to finance_engine
Before deploying an index, measure whether it retrieves the right nodes for your target questions. LlamaIndex provides

from llama_index.core.evaluation import RetrieverEvaluator, generate_question_context_pairs
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex

# Step 1: Generate (question, relevant_node_id) pairs from your nodes
# generate_question_context_pairs calls the LLM to create questions per node
qa_dataset = generate_question_context_pairs(
    nodes=index.docstore.docs.values(),  # TextNode objects
    llm=Settings.llm,
    num_questions_per_chunk=2,
)

# Step 2: Evaluate the retriever
retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=5)
evaluator = RetrieverEvaluator.from_metric_names(
    ["mrr", "hit_rate"],
    retriever=retriever,
)

results = await evaluator.aevaluate_dataset(qa_dataset)
print(f"MRR: {results.metric_vals_dict['mrr']:.3f}")
print(f"Hit rate @5: {results.metric_vals_dict['hit_rate']:.3f}")
stores embeddings (FAISS, Qdrant, Chroma, Pinecone, Weaviate…)
stores raw node text and metadata (SimpleDocumentStore, MongoDB…)
stores index metadata — which nodes belong to which index
By default all three use in-memory implementations that can be persisted to JSON on disk. For production, configure persistent backends for each:

# pip install llama-index-vector-stores-chroma chromadb
import chromadb
from llama_index.vector_stores.chroma import ChromaVectorStore
from llama_index.core import StorageContext, VectorStoreIndex

chroma_client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")
chroma_collection = chroma_client.get_or_create_collection("my_docs")
vector_store = ChromaVectorStore(chroma_collection=chroma_collection)

# Doc store and index store stay on disk by default
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(
    vector_store=vector_store,
)

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    storage_context=storage_context,
)

# On restart: reload without re-embedding
chroma_collection2 = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")            .get_collection("my_docs")
vector_store2 = ChromaVectorStore(chroma_collection2)
index2 = VectorStoreIndex.from_vector_store(vector_store2)
backed by a persistent store (Qdrant, Chroma, Pinecone). Switch to
when your queries require multi-hop relationship traversal.
